1:
The chapter opens with David a fugitive from Naioth, and the witnesses gather around three things: the flight, the friendship, and the protest of innocence. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Charles Ellicott reads the strange events among the prophetic schools at Ramah as a warning to David that even the home of his old master Samuel was no permanent sanctuary; yet the interval gave him time to fly and take counsel with his loved friend, the king's son. It was, Ellicott supposes, by Samuel's advice that he secretly betook himself once more to the city of Saul, and, alone with Jonathan, passionately asserted his entire innocence of the crimes the jealous king had laid to his charge. He hears in David's words the very substance of not a few of the Psalms, where in touching language David maintains how bitterly the world had wronged a righteous, innocent man.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the most of the friendship. It was happy for David that he had such a friend at court when he had such an enemy on the throne: a friend that loveth at all times, and a brother that is born for adversity (Prov. 17:17). Jonathan loved David as well now in his distress as he had done in his triumph (1 Sam. 18:1). David appeals to him with a plain What have I done?, ready to humble himself and beg pardon for any real offence; and when Jonathan, from filial respect, is loath to believe his father capable of such wickedness, David swears the peace upon Saul, protesting that there is but a step between me and death. Henry turns it to comfort: the trials David met with prepared him for future advancement, for the Lord does not put those whom he prepares for glory into immediate possession of the kingdom, but leads them to it through much tribulation.

The reformed John Gill fixes the moment precisely, noting that David fled while Saul was prophesying or lay bound in a trance at Naioth. Beneath their differing emphases the voices are one: a guiltless man, hunted without cause, seeks out the one heart at court that still loves him, and Henry draws the tender parallel that so binds the chapter to the gospel: as Jonathan freely offered his service, so Christ testifies his love to us: Ask, and it shall be done for you; and we must testify ours to him by keeping his commandments.
2:
Jonathan's reply, God forbid, thou shalt not die, becomes for the witnesses a study in a loyal son's reluctance to think evil of his father, and they read his confidence as sincere but mistaken. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the ground of it. Keil and Delitzsch render his effort to pacify David: Far be it! my father does nothing great or small that he does not reveal to me; why should he hide this thing from me? It is not so. They note the textual delicacy, that the Hebrew lo ('to him') after behold stands for lo ('not'), and prefer the reading that Saul does nothing without showing it to his son. Charles Ellicott draws out the psychology: the princely Jonathan even now refuses to believe that his loved father, when he was himself, really wished ill to David, and puts all that had happened down to Saul's unhappy malady. He urges that if the king in good earnest had designs on David's life, he would in his calm, lucid days have consulted Jonathan, to whom he confided all his state secrets. Ellicott adds that the versions here agree with the commentators in reading not for to him, an aleph substituted for a vau.

The wesleyan-arminian voices supply the reason for Jonathan's trust. Adam Clarke observes that Jonathan thought his father could have no evil design against David because of the oath Saul had sworn to him (1 Sam. 19:6), and at any rate would do nothing without informing his son. John Wesley, brief as ever, takes the closing Is it not so? the same way: Jonathan gave credit to his father's oath.

The reformed John Gill gathers the whole together. Jonathan could not believe his father had any such intention; if Saul had betrayed anything of the kind, it was only when a frenzy and melancholy disorder had seized him; the thing was too gross and detestable to be credited. The witnesses do not divide, but they mark a poignant distance between the two friends: Jonathan, guileless and dutiful, covers his father's shame as far as justice allows, while David, who has looked deeper into Saul's heart, knows that the sworn peace of 1 Sam. 19:6 has already been broken.
3:
David answers Jonathan's confidence with an oath, and the witnesses concentrate on his deeper reading of Saul and on the famous measure of his peril. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work. Keil and Delitzsch render the sworn words: Thy father knoweth that I have found favour in thine eyes, and thinketh, Jonathan shall not know this, lest he be grieved; but truly, as Jehovah liveth and thy soul liveth, there is hardly a step between me and death. They note that the Hebrew ki introduces the substance of the oath, as in 1 Sam. 14:44, and that David had looked deeper into Saul's heart than his friend. Charles Ellicott presses the same point: David, with his clear, bright intellect, saw further than the heroic, guileless son, recognizing only too vividly the intensity of the king's hatred; and the mournful earnestness of the son of Jesse had its effect upon the prince, who consented to the public trial his friend asked.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lingers on the phrase there is but a step between me and death, calling David's life in the most imminent danger, and finds the very figure in Juvenal, who mocked those who for gain trust their lives to a plank, four or seven fingers from death. John Wesley explains the form: the matter being of great moment, and Jonathan doubting its truth, David confirms his word with an oath, first interposing a reason why Saul had concealed the design from his son.

The reformed John Gill reads the oath as pastoral strategy: David, having no doubt himself, swore to remove any doubt that might remain in Jonathan, who was unwilling to believe his father capable of so foul a crime. The witnesses agree without dispute that David has correctly diagnosed the situation the trusting Jonathan cannot yet see; and the proverb-like weight of but a step between me and death becomes, in their hands, a word for every believer, that at all times life hangs by a thread and we should be prepared for the event, yet cannot die till the purpose of God concerning us is accomplished.
4:
Jonathan's open-handed answer, Whatsoever thy soul desireth, I will even do it for thee, the witnesses read as the very definition of friendship and as the turning point at which the trusting son at last credits his friend's fear. The reformed John Gill marks the change of heart: Jonathan now gives credit to what David has said, and speaks in order to comfort and support him under the apprehensions of danger he had felt. The blank cheque of love is offered not carelessly but because Jonathan has been persuaded that the peril is real.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the setting into which the offer flows. When Jonathan said, What thy soul saith, will I do to thee, promising to fulfil every wish, David made his request: Behold, to-morrow is new moon, and I ought to sit and eat with the king; let me go, that I may conceal myself in the field till the third evening. Keil draws from this the historical inference that Saul kept a feast at the new moon, so that the day was not merely the religious festival of the law (Num. 10:10; Num. 28:11) but a civil festival kept for two days; for David reckons to the third evening and proposes to hide himself on the new moon's day and the day following, a point confirmed, they add, by the later verses where Saul expects David at table on both days.

Though only two witnesses speak here, the verse is a hinge on which the whole plot swings. Jonathan's I will even do it for thee is, as the puritan-evangelical reading elsewhere in the chapter observes, true friendship that needs no proviso of lawful and honest, since he knew David too well to fear he would ask anything otherwise. Christ testifies the same love to his own: Ask, and it shall be done for you (compare John 16:23); and the covenant of friendship here becomes a mirror of the covenant of grace, in which a willing love binds itself in advance to the good of the beloved. The witnesses do not contend; they honour a devotion that asks no conditions before it promises.
5:
David unfolds his plan of trial, and the witnesses attend to the new moon, to the reckoning of days, and to the shrewdness of the test. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the festival. Adam Clarke notes that the Hebrew months were lunar and reckoned from new moon to new moon, so that the first appearance of each was scrupulously observed with sacrifices and a feast (Num. 10:10; Num. 28:11), the families feasting together, though the great gathering of a whole tribe came but once a year. Charles Ellicott paints the court's observance: the feast was kept carefully, doubtless with the blast of trumpets and with burnt and sin offerings, and was joined to a state and family banquet at which David, as the king's son-in-law and a high officer of the army, was expected to appear. Ellicott catches the tragic difference between the friends: Jonathan still hoped that after the seizure at Ramah Saul would return and treat David with the confidence of old days, while David, convinced that there was but a step between him and death, would not trust himself to Saul's hands until the experiment had been made.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render David's request to hide in the field unto the third day at even, and read the plan as turning on the vacant seat, which would betray the king's mind. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley clarifies the timing: to the third day means the next day but one after the new moon, and David did not mean to lie hidden in one place all three days, but to secure himself at Bethlehem or elsewhere until then.

The reformed John Gill keeps the purpose in view: David was moved to propose this method as a trial of the disposition of Saul's mind toward him. The witnesses agree that the design is sound and natural. As the puritan reading of the chapter observes, Saul, though rejected of God and forsaken by his Spirit, still kept up the holy feasts; and there may be, the same reading warns, the remains of external devotion where there is nothing but the ruins of real virtue. Into that hollow piety David sends his empty seat as a searching question.
6:
The Bethlehem excuse David puts in Jonathan's mouth raises the one genuine moral debate of the chapter: was it true, or a feigned pretext, and does Scripture approve it? The grammatical-historical commentators press the harder reading. Charles Ellicott holds that the Mosaic law (Deut. 12:5) required such sacrificial feasts at the tabernacle, but that since the ruin of Shiloh they were kept in tribal centres and family usages; and though the yearly feast was probably being held at Bethlehem, David did not purpose to be present, so that the excuse was a feigned one. Ellicott is careful to add that the morality of the request is by no means sanctioned by the compiler of the history, who simply relates the story. Keil and Delitzsch, commenting a verse earlier on the same words, put it bluntly: we see from these words that David did not look upon prevarication as a sin.

Against this stands the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who, summarizing the plan, judges that we have reason to think the excuse was true: David really was invited by his elder brother to Bethlehem to keep the new moon with his relations, for besides the monthly solemnity they had a yearly sacrifice and a holy feast for all the family. Henry even draws an edifying conclusion, that David's was a very religious family, a house that had a church in it. Where Ellicott and Keil see a pious cover for a real absence, Henry sees a genuine invitation honestly pleaded.

The reformed John Gill glosses the opening words simply, if thy father at all miss me, that is, diligently inquire after me, and does not moralize. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds only that Jonathan, as the king's son and deputy, was accustomed to grant military men leave to depart for a season on just occasions, so that the request lay well within his power to allow. The witnesses thus leave the reader with a real and unresolved tension: whether David's word was strictly true or a permitted stratagem in mortal danger, and whether the sacred writer, in recording, means to commend. What none of them doubts is that the danger which drove him to it was real.
7:
David lays down the logic of his test, and the witnesses weigh both its clarity and a subtle question about how sure David really was. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the two outcomes: if thy father says, It is well, there is peace to thy servant; but if he be very wroth, know that evil is determined by him. They gloss the verb rendered determined as meaning to be completed, hence firmly and unalterably resolved (compare 1 Sam. 25:17; Esther 7:7). Then they stage a small disagreement among expositors: Sebastian Schmidt inferred from the closing words that the fact of Saul's murderous intent was certain to David though not to Jonathan, while Thenius argued, more correctly in their judgement, that it is perfectly obvious from this that David was not quite clear as to Saul's intentions. Keil sides with the reading that leaves David genuinely uncertain, needing the trial as much as Jonathan did.

The reformed John Gill reads the first branch warmly: if he say thus, It is well, that is, it is very good and right in him to do so, David would take Saul's easy acceptance of the excuse as a sign that the king's mind was at peace toward him. The whole verse turns on the difference between calm dispensing and hot resentment, and Gill lets the two responses stand as a clean diagnostic of the heart.

Only two witnesses comment directly, but their agreement is instructive: the wrath, not the words, will be the tell. As the puritan reading of the chapter puts it, if Saul admitted the excuse and dispensed with David's absence, one might conclude he was reconciled; but if he was thrown into a passion by it, it was easy to conclude he designed a mischief, since he did not love David so well as to desire his presence for any innocent end. The test is shrewdly built on this, that a calm man can conceal his purpose behind courteous words, but an angry man cannot; provocation strips the disguise from a settled hatred. David has framed a question that Saul must answer not with his tongue, which can lie, but with his temper, which cannot. The verse thus rests the trial on a moral certainty: hatred, provoked, cannot hide itself, and a passion kindled over an empty chair will betray a heart already resolved on blood.
8:
David presses his plea on the ground of the covenant, and the witnesses dwell on the sacredness of that bond and on the startling offer, slay me thyself. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the appeal: David could be sure Jonathan would grant the request because Jonathan had brought him into a covenant of Jehovah (1 Sam. 18:3), so called because it was made with a solemn invocation of the Lord; and to make doubly sure, David added, if there is a fault in me, do thou kill me; for why wilt thou bring me to thy father? Charles Ellicott pauses over the intimacy of the record and asks how the compiler knew what passed when David was alone with Jonathan; following Ewald, he suggests that David, drawing Jonathan's posterity to his court in after years, himself told them of these last events. Ellicott also frames the pastoral note that this hour was the turning point of a grand career, from which David's way became for a long, weary period the road of almost uninterrupted suffering, the royal road to true greatness. On slay me thyself he cites Lange, that this supposes Jonathan had the right to inflict capital punishment for crimes against his father as king.

The wesleyan-arminian voices sharpen the two clauses. Adam Clarke reads David's offer as fearless honesty: if Jonathan sees him plotting against the state or the king's life, let him slay him himself. John Wesley defines a covenant of the Lord as a solemn covenant, not lightly undertaken but seriously entered in the name and fear of God, who is called to witness the sincerity of it and to avenge the perfidy of any who break it; and he glosses the closing question, why shouldest thou bring me to thy father, as, why betray me by concealing his evil intentions from me?

The reformed John Gill keeps the practical aim before us: David asks Jonathan to deal kindly by informing him how his father's mind stood, that he might govern himself accordingly, either appearing at court or providing for his safety by flight. The witnesses agree that David stakes everything on a conscience and a covenant: he will not urge a friend to shield him in a wrong, and so he offers his own neck if guilt can be found, knowing there is none.
9:
Jonathan's indignant Far be it from thee the witnesses read as an outburst of wounded loyalty, and they attend to the broken Hebrew, the oath implied, and the great renewal of covenant that unfolds from it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the protest and mark its form: the words are an asseveration on oath in which the formula must be supplied, so that Jonathan means, if I certainly discover that evil is determined by my father against thee, and do not tell thee, may God do so to me and more. They prefer this to reading the last clause as a question. Charles Ellicott hears the emotion in the grammar: the strong absit hoc a te bears emphatic testimony to Jonathan's belief in his friend's stainless loyalty; the sentences are broken, the next left incomplete in the Hebrew, betokening the agitation and deep feeling of the chivalrous, indignant speaker.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest account, treating the verse as the seed of everything that follows. Jonathan solemnly renews his friendship, promising to make the matter neither better nor worse than it is: if there be good toward thee, I will show it thee; if evil, I will send thee away. He appeals to God as witness and as judge, the Lord do so and much more to Jonathan if he speak deceitfully, that David might have strong consolation, as God has confirmed his promises to us (Heb. 6:17). Henry follows the covenant as Jonathan entails it upon his posterity, out of firm belief in David's advancement and pure love, for he loved him as he loved his own soul; he fears lest David's house should one day do to his seed as Abimelech did to the sons of Gideon (Judg. 9:5), and secures it by the appeal, the Lord be between me and thee for ever. It was in remembrance of this, Henry adds, that David was afterward kind to Mephibosheth (2 Sam. 9:7).

The reformed John Gill reads the disavowal simply: far be it from Jonathan to entertain such a thought, or to have the least suspicion that he would conceal his father's ill intentions if he knew them. The voices do not divide; together they show a friendship that binds itself with oaths because true love is not content to be trusted, but seeks to be sworn.
10:
David's questions, Who shall tell me? or what if thy father answer thee roughly?, the witnesses read as the practical prudence of a hunted man who foresees how suspicion will cut off ordinary channels of news. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the sense favoured by De Wette and Maurer: who will tell me how thy father expresses himself concerning me, or what will thy father answer thee roughly, if thou shouldst attempt to tell me thyself? They reject the rendering of the little Hebrew word as if perchance, arguing that even where such a sense is possible the meaning or still lies beneath it. The questions, they add, sprang from a correct estimate of the circumstances: Saul's suspicions would lead him to conclude that there was an understanding between Jonathan and David, and he would take steps to keep Jonathan from carrying David word of the outcome. Charles Ellicott agrees that the original is here very abrupt and involved, the very words of the excited and sorrowful friends remembered and reported; he offers the alternative, if thy father shall answer thee harshly, who will declare it to me?, and looks ahead to the field where they will fix a secret sign.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the plain force of the first question: who shall give me the necessary information? what means wilt thou use to convey this intelligence to me? The difficulty is not distrust of Jonathan but the danger that a watched court will leave no safe messenger.

The reformed John Gill keeps the question concrete: David asks who shall tell him the disposition of Saul's mind, whether the king gave a kind answer to Jonathan's report. The witnesses agree there is no debate of substance here, only a shared recognition of the peril of communication under a suspicious king, which is precisely why Jonathan will now propose a wordless sign in the open field, safe from every prying ear. The verse thus turns the narrative from what shall be learned to how it shall be conveyed, and prepares for the covenant and the arrows that follow.
11:
Jonathan's invitation, Come, and let us go out into the field, the witnesses read as a move into privacy where covenant and counsel can be safely renewed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the purpose: before replying to David's questions, Jonathan took him to the field so that they might there fix upon the sign by which he could give word of Saul's mind in a way that would rouse no suspicion. In that open place, free from every observer, Jonathan first renewed his covenant with David by swearing to inform him of his father's feelings, then entreated him, with a certain presentiment that David would one day be king, to keep his love toward his house for ever, and finally made David swear again concerning his love before giving the sign. Adam Clarke reads the verse the same way, that in answer to David's who shall tell me? Jonathan now shows him how the intelligence shall be conveyed.

The reformed John Gill supplies the plain reason for the walk: they went out that they might talk more fully, freely, and familiarly of this affair, without danger of being overheard by the servants of Saul in the palace where they then were. The field is chosen not for its beauty but for its silence.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the moment toward its highest meaning. Jonathan faithfully promises to let David know how he finds his father affected; and it will be kindness to ourselves and ours, Henry urges, to secure an interest in those whom God favours and to make his friends ours. True friendship, he adds, rests on a firm basis and is able to silence ambition, self-love, and undue regard for others. But then he turns from the friend to the Friend: who can fully understand the love of Jesus, who gave himself as a sacrifice for rebellious, polluted sinners? how great then ought to be the force and effects of our love to him, to his cause, and his people! The witnesses do not differ; a quiet errand into a field becomes, in their hands, a parable of the love that seeks a private place to bind itself to the beloved, and a foreshadowing of the greater love that laid down its life (compare John 15:13).
12:
Jonathan opens his oath with O Lord God of Israel, and the witnesses find here a real textual puzzle knotted into a solemn vow. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the difficulty plainly: something is evidently wanting in the verse. The Septuagint reads The Lord God of Israel doth know; the Syriac and Arabic, The Lord God of Israel is witness; while two of Kennicott's manuscripts supply the word liveth, giving As the Lord God of Israel liveth, when I have sounded my father... the Lord do so and much more to Jonathan, which Clarke thinks makes the best sense of all. Charles Ellicott agrees that the bare vocative of the English Version has generally been thought an impossible rendering, there being no analogy for such a mode of address, and notes that the versions avoid it by supplying a verb. Ellicott also fixes the reckoning of time, to-morrow, or the third day: the present day, the morrow which is the new moon, and the day after which counts as the third; and he draws out the plan, that if the news were good Jonathan would send a special messenger, but if the king showed enmity he would come himself and see David for the last time, this sad message should be brought by no messenger.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch connect this verse with the next and read the opening as a solemn invocation, Jehovah, God of Israel!, introducing the oath, with neither is witness nor knoweth to be supplied, the exclamation itself carrying the force of the vow.

The reformed John Gill takes the same line as the Jewish commentators Jarchi and Kimchi, that the words are the form of an oath, by the Lord God of Israel I swear unto thee. Beneath the disagreement over the missing word the witnesses are entirely at one on the sense: Jonathan binds himself by the covenant God to sound his father and to bring David a faithful report, whether of peace or of danger. The corruption in the letter has not obscured the transparency of the vow.
13:
Jonathan invokes the direst curse upon himself if he should fail David, and the witnesses hear in the same breath a remarkable confession that the kingdom will pass to his friend. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the sworn promise: the Lord do so and much more to Jonathan; but if it please my father to do thee evil, then I will show it thee and send thee away, that thou mayest go in peace; and the Lord be with thee, as he hath been with my father. They carry the oath straight on into the entail of the covenant that follows, whereby Jonathan binds David to show kindness to his house for ever, and where the Lord required it at the hand of David's enemies.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the closing clause, the Lord be with thee, as he hath been with my father, and draws the striking inference: from this and other passages here it is evident that Jonathan knew that the Lord had appointed David to the kingdom. The wish is thus more than affection; it is a deliberate transfer of the divine benediction from the failing house of Saul to the rising house of David, spoken by the very heir who stands to lose the crown.

The reformed John Gill weighs the self-imprecation: the Lord do so and much more to Jonathan, that is, recompense him with evil more than can be thought or expressed, should he neglect to inform David of a good disposition in Saul toward him. The oath is not lightly framed; Jonathan stakes himself upon it. Though few witnesses speak, their agreement is weighty and moving. Jonathan swears to warn and to send away, to secure not his own throne but his friend's safety, and to bless David with the presence of God that had once rested on Saul and was now departing. The heir prays the crown onto another's head and asks in return only that his children be spared, so pure and so clear-eyed is his love. Here, the witnesses agree, is faith submitting to the counsel of God even at the cost of a dynasty.
14:
Jonathan turns from David's safety to his own children's future, and the witnesses read his broken, urgent words as the plea of a man who already foresees his house made desolate. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Hebrew of this verse and the next is again very confused, abrupt, and ungrammatical, and attributes it to the violent emotion of the speaker, whose very words David long remembered with a sad accuracy. Following the Syriac and Arabic, and with Maurer, Ewald, Keil, and Lange, he changes and not into the wish O that, reading: mayest thou, if I still live, show me the favour of the Lord, and if I die, not withdraw thy favour from my house for ever, not even when Jehovah shall cut off the enemies of David, every one from the face of the earth. Those last words, Ellicott observes, tell with striking clearness how thoroughly convinced Jonathan was that David's cause, as the cause of their God, would surely triumph; mournfully he looked on to his father's downfall and his own premature death, and bespoke his friend's kindness for children who would before long be landless, homeless orphans.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke puts it as a plain conditional: when thou comest to the kingdom, if I am alive, thou shalt show kindness to me, and continue it to my family after me. John Wesley defines the kindness of the Lord as that to which David had engaged himself in the covenant sworn between them in God's presence, and reads that I die not as a prayer that David not cut off him or his, as princes of a new line were wont to destroy the nearest relations of the house from which the kingdom had passed.

The reformed John Gill notes a fine point: Jonathan did not doubt the kindness would be shown him while he lived, and so did not make that a request, but pressed it rather for his house after his death. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the tender realism of Jonathan's faith: sure that God will exalt David and cut off his enemies, he does not grasp at the throne but only pleads that grace, not the sword, may meet his orphans when that day comes.
15:
This short clause, thou shalt not cut off thy kindness from my house for ever, is carried by a single voice, and the witnesses' brevity here matches the verse's own compression of a great request into a few words. The reformed John Gill gives the plain sense: Jonathan asks that his family should partake of David's kindness as well as himself, so that the covenant reaches beyond the two friends to their seed. The pledge is not for a season but for ever, and Gill's short gloss lets the weight of that word stand.

Though only Gill comments directly, the verse cannot be read apart from the surrounding oath, and the wider company of witnesses illumines what he states so briefly. The grammatical-historical reading of the neighbouring verses hears in not even when Jehovah shall cut off the enemies of David every one from the face of the earth a settled certainty that David will reign and his foes fall, so that this clause is Jonathan's provision against the very day of David's total victory, when a new king might be tempted to secure his throne by destroying the old line. The wesleyan-arminian reading of the covenant defines the kindness sought as the kindness of the Lord, the covenanted grace that God himself shows to those he takes into fellowship with him and to their seed after them.

So the verse, small as it is, gathers up the pathos of the whole interview. Jonathan, heir to a doomed dynasty, does not ask to keep the crown; he asks only that mercy outlive him. He binds the future king by an everlasting bond to spare his children when power and prudence would counsel otherwise. The witnesses agree, without any dispute, that this is love reaching past the grave, and that its fulfilment lies far ahead in David's tenderness to the lame son of Jonathan (compare 2 Sam. 9:7), when the covenant sworn in a field was honoured at a king's table. What flesh and self-interest would have cut off, covenant love preserved. There is a deeper likeness the witnesses cannot but feel: the kindness Jonathan begs is called the kindness of the Lord, and it is the very pattern of the covenant God keeps with his own, extending his mercy not to a man only but to his seed after him, so that the friendship of these two becomes a small and shining image of the everlasting covenant of grace. Jonathan asks for his house exactly what God pledges to his people, that the kindness begun in love shall never be cut off.
16:
The verse records the covenant sealed, So Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David, and the witnesses attend to its scope, its grammar, and the imprecation attached to it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the English Version's supplied saying, holding it better to read the verse as a remark interposed by the narrator, and to translate literally, So Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David, and Jehovah required it at the hand of David's enemies. On this reading the closing clause is not part of Jonathan's speech but the historian's own note that God did in time avenge the covenant upon those who set themselves against David.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley marks the enlargement: the covenant that before was personal, between the two friends, Jonathan now extends to the whole house of David, expecting a reciprocal enlargement on David's side, which, Wesley adds, he doubtless obtained. He reads the imprecation as a solemn sanction: if either Jonathan or any of his house should break this covenant and prove enemies to David or his house, let the Lord, the witness of the covenant, severely punish the violators of it.

The reformed John Gill underlines the same widening, that the covenant was made not with David only, which was now renewed, but with his family also. Though the witnesses differ slightly over whether the last clause is Jonathan's oath or the narrator's comment, they agree entirely on the substance: a private friendship has become a dynastic bond, sworn before God and guarded by his justice. As the puritan reading of the chapter observes, true friends cannot but covet to transmit to those after them their mutual affections, and this covenant, called the kindness of the Lord, is of the very sort God shows to those he takes into covenant with himself, being a God to them and to their seed. The little verse thus stands as the hinge of the friendship: the love of two men, translated into an oath that would outlast them both, and answered in the end by the God who required it at the hand of David's enemies.
17:
Jonathan makes David swear yet again, and the witnesses find the motive named in the text itself, because he loved him as he loved his own soul. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, Jonathan adjured David again by his love to him, because he loved him as his own soul (compare 1 Sam. 18:1), that is, he once more implored David most earnestly, with an oath, to show favour to him and his house. They carry the thought straight into the appointed sign, noting that Jonathan next tells David he will be missed at the feast, his seat empty at Saul's table.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the poignancy of the repeated oath: throughout this touching interview it is the prince who appears as the suppliant for the outlaw's future kindness. Looking forward with absolute certainty to the day when his persecuted friend would sit on the throne and he himself lie in the grave, Jonathan dreaded for his own fatherless children the fate that too probably awaited them, since it had in all ages been a common custom in the East, when a dynasty was violently changed, to put to death the children and near relations of the former king. The insistence on a second oath is thus not distrust but dread, the anxious love of a father for the orphans he foresees.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley explains the Hebrew idiom, that Jonathan added or proceeded to make David swear; having himself sworn in the foregoing verse, he now requires David's oath by way of restipulation and confirmation, desiring, out of true friendship, that the covenant might be inviolably observed through all their generations. The reformed John Gill reads it the same way, that Jonathan, having sworn to keep covenant with David and his family, insisted David swear to keep covenant with him and his. The witnesses do not divide; they are struck, with one accord, by the spectacle of the king's son begging the fugitive shepherd for mercy on his line, and they find the ground of it stated with beautiful simplicity in the love that made David's life as precious to Jonathan as his own.
18:
Jonathan turns to the practical arrangement, To-morrow is the new moon, and thou shalt be missed, because thy seat will be empty, and the witnesses read it as the calm beginning of the appointed test. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that the empty place at Saul's table is the whole mechanism of the sign, and carry the discussion into the next verse, where the Hebrew grows difficult. On the third day come down quickly and go to the place where thou didst hide thyself on the day of the deed, and place thyself by the stone Ezel, they concede the phrase rendered on the third day cannot be secured by parallel passages but is established by the ordinal the third and by Arabic usage, while the word rendered quickly, literally go violently down, is more striking still, yet defended by the Septuagint and Vulgate readings.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott voices Jonathan's resolve with feeling: after the passionate conclusion of the covenant, the prince returns to the plan, the last trial shall be as you propose. At the state banquet of my father tomorrow your seat, as agreed, will be empty; then you and I, when King Saul misses you, will know the worst. The empty chair, so lately David's rightful place as the king's son-in-law, becomes the silent question by which the king's heart will be read.

The reformed John Gill simply anchors the day, that the morrow is the new moon, the first day of the month, as David had already observed. Only three witnesses comment, and they do not disagree; together they let the quiet practicality of the verse stand in sober contrast to the storm it will provoke. Everything now waits on the vacant seat. As the puritan reading of the chapter notes, David's place did not use to be empty at a sacred feast, for none was more content than he in attending holy duties; he absents himself now only because he must come at the peril of his life, and self-preservation obliges him to withdraw. The test is set; the friends have done all they can; the next move belongs to Saul.
19:
The details of the rendezvous crowd this verse with textual difficulty, and the grammatical-historical commentators do the patient work. Charles Ellicott warns that quickly does not faithfully render the Hebrew m'od, which comes from the Vulgate; the literal sense is greatly, and Dean Payne Smith's go a long way down into the valley probably catches the meaning, the point being not speed but distance, that David should be far off where no prying eye might catch sight of him. The phrase when the business was in hand Ellicott finds hard, preferring with Gesenius, De Wette, and Maurer the literal on the day of the deed, the deed being Saul's design of killing David (compare 1 Sam. 19:2). Of the stone Ezel, mentioned nowhere else, he records the conjecture from the Speaker's Commentary that the Septuagint's argab, a heap of stones, may be the true reading, so that David's hiding place was a cavernous rock or ancient ruin. Keil and Delitzsch render the whole arrangement and note that Jonathan will shoot arrows to the side of it, as if aiming at a mark, the definite article suggesting either a thing already familiar or the arrows in his quiver.

The reformed John Gill keeps the practical thread: David is to stay three days from court, at Bethlehem or elsewhere incognito, and not in the field itself, where for want of food he could not remain so long. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley glosses the two hard phrases in the same direction, that when the business was in hand means when this same business of discovering Saul's mind was formerly in agitation (compare 1 Sam. 19:2), and that the stone Ezel was one that directed travellers in the way, near some cave or convenient place.

The witnesses do not divide over meaning so much as labour together over an obscure text, and their combined effort yields a clear enough picture: David is to withdraw far off to a known hiding place by a landmark stone, and wait there through the days of the feast until Jonathan comes with a wordless sign. The very difficulty of the Hebrew, Ellicott suggests, is itself a mark of authenticity, the tangled record of an anxious plan laid in haste and remembered ever after.
20:
The sign of the three arrows is fixed, and the witnesses describe its ingenious simplicity. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the whole device: David was to stay at the stone Ezel, where there was probably some cave or hiding place; to avoid all suspicion Jonathan would not go to him but take a servant into the fields and pretend to exercise himself in archery, shooting three arrows the better to cover his design. If he then told the lad who fetched them, the arrows are on this side of thee, it would be a sign that David might safely return to court, no evil being intended; but if he said, the arrows are beyond thee, then David must escape for his life, Saul having determined his destruction.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads it as a very simple sign that seems to speak of very early primitive times, and notes that Jonathan slightly varies from his original purpose. In the earlier plan it seemed he meant to send a special messenger if the news were good; now the arrangement is that in either event he will come himself out from the city into the solitary valley where David lies hid by Ezel. He describes the mechanics: the prince would post his young armour-bearer as a marker, shoot, and then call out the fatal or the friendly words, having taken these precautions in case he should be accompanied by friends of his father, when the wordless sign alone would have to serve. As it fell out, Jonathan was able to come alone, use the sign to attract his friend's attention, and then meet him for the last sorrowful parting.

The reformed John Gill adds the neat correspondence that fixes the number: three arrows were chosen according to the three days David was to be missing. The witnesses do not dispute; they admire together a plan at once childlike and shrewd, a game of archery made the vehicle of life and death, and they note the slight, honest discrepancy between Jonathan's first thought of a messenger and his final resolve to come himself, a change that only deepens the tenderness of the scene, since even good news he would not now entrust to another's mouth.
21:
Jonathan spells out the friendly half of the sign, and the witnesses read it as the token of peace and safety. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the arrangement closely: Behold, I will send the boy, saying, Go, get the arrows. If I say to the boy, Behold, the arrows are on this side of thee, fetch them; then come, for peace is to thee, and it is nothing, as truly as Jehovah liveth. They note how the interjection behold directs attention to the main point, and that the whole appointment was as simple as it was suited to its purpose. The arrows falling short of the lad, within call, would tell David that Saul's mind was calm and he might return without fear.

The reformed John Gill attends to the boy himself, the lad who attended Jonathan, carried his bow and arrows, and fetched them when they were shot, an ordinary servant who could be used as an unwitting instrument of the secret. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the practical detail that Jonathan would send the lad out before shooting, to find and take up the arrows, and would then shoot either short of him or beyond him as occasion required, so that the direction of the shot, not any spoken message, carried the news.

Only three witnesses speak, and they do not differ; they dwell together on the safeguard of the plan, that a boy who knew nothing could be trusted to carry an arrow but never a secret. The oath sealed into the friendly words, as the Lord liveth, shows how much hung on this device: it was not a mere game but a matter of a man's life, and Jonathan bound even his reassurance with the name of God. The witnesses note that the very simplicity is its safety; nothing in the scene would rouse a watcher's suspicion, yet everything David needed to know could be told in the flight of an arrow and a shouted word about a careless shot. Peace or flight would be spoken over the head of an innocent child who understood neither.
22:
The other half of the sign spells danger and flight, and the witnesses read it as the word that would send David from home for good. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, But if I say to the youth, Behold, the arrows are beyond thee, then go, for Jehovah sendeth thee away, that is, bids thee flee; and they repeat that the appointment of this sign was as simple as it was suited to the purpose. The striking phrase is Jonathan's, that it is the Lord who sends David away: the flight is not merely Saul's driving out but God's own providence removing his anointed to safety, a thought Jonathan lays like a benediction over the hardest word of all.

Keil carries the reading on into the covenant clause that follows, and as touching the matter which thou and I have spoken of, behold, the Lord be between thee and me for ever, explaining that this arrangement was to remain an eternal secret between them, the Lord standing as witness and judge should either break the covenant, after the manner of the pillar and heap in Gen. 31:48. The word, he adds, refers not merely to the sign but to the whole matter, including the renewal of the bond of friendship.

The reformed John Gill gives the plain sense of the danger signal, that if Jonathan said the arrows were beyond the young man, being shot to a greater distance than where he stood, David would know the worst. Only two witnesses comment here, and there is no dispute between them; they let the two clauses stand together, the fatal sign and the eternal covenant, so that the same breath that would banish David also binds the friends for ever. The witnesses feel the pathos of it: the arrow that flies past the boy carries not only a warning but a farewell, and Jonathan, unable to soften the danger, wraps it in the assurance that even David's exile is the sending of the Lord, and that whatever distance now divides them, God will keep the peace of their covenant between them and their seed while the world stands.
23:
The friends commit their whole compact to God, and the witnesses read this verse as the sealing clause that lifts the private arrangement into a sacred and perpetual bond. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, And as for the word that we have spoken, I and thou, behold, the Lord is between me and thee for ever, and explain the ellipsis: the Lord stands as witness and judge in case either should break the covenant, on the pattern of the pillar and heap of witness in Gen. 31:48. There is no need, they add, to suppose a word for witness has dropped from the text; the sense is contained in the words themselves. And the word, they insist, refers not merely to the sign just agreed but to the whole matter, including the renewal of the bond of friendship.

The reformed John Gill takes the same view, that the matter which thou and I have spoken of is the covenant they had made between them and their families. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley unfolds the invocation, that the Lord is called upon as a witness and a judge between us and our families for ever, if on either side this league of friendship be violated.

Only three witnesses speak, and they are wholly agreed; together they show how deeply these men felt that a covenant of love was a covenant with God in the midst. The friends do not trust to memory or to sentiment but place the living God between them, so that their kindness to each other becomes a kind of worship, and any breach of it a sin against heaven. As the puritan reading of the chapter observes, when they parted Jonathan concluded with just this solemn appeal, that God himself be judge between their houses for ever; and it was in remembrance of this covenant, sealed under the eye of God, that David was afterward kind to Mephibosheth (compare 2 Sam. 21:7). The witnesses find in the verse a lasting lesson, that the surest bond between friends is the God who stands between them, at once the witness of their vows and the avenger of their treachery.
24:
The scene shifts to the feast, and the witnesses read the vacant place at the king's table as the quiet fuse of the coming explosion. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the stage: David hid himself in the field, while Jonathan endeavoured, as agreed, to apologise for his absence; and on the new moon's day Saul sat at table in his customary seat by the wall, the place of honour, as in eastern lands the place of honour is the seat in the corner. They wrestle with the difficult clause and Jonathan rose up, and Abner sat by Saul's side, taking it to mean that when Abner entered Jonathan rose from his place beside Saul and gave it up to him, though the text leaves unsaid to what seat Jonathan then moved.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the feast a mirror of Saul's ruined soul. The king sat down to feast on the peace offerings with his heart as full of envy and malice against David as it could hold; he should first have been reconciled and then offered his gift, but instead he hoped at this very feast to drink the blood of David. Henry marvels at the abomination of a sacrifice brought with such a mind (compare Prov. 21:27), and reads David's absence as forced, not careless: none was more content than he in attending holy duties, and he stayed away only because he must have come at the peril of his life, self-preservation obliging him to withdraw, as Christ himself often absconded till his hour was come. In imminent peril, Henry allows, present opportunities may be waived; we ought not to throw ourselves into the mouth of danger.

The reformed John Gill notes that David hid himself not at once but at the appointed time, having gone first to Bethlehem and returned; and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that he hid at the time appointed, going first to Bethlehem and thence back to the field as occasion required. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds only that the Septuagint paraphrases sat him down as came to the table. The witnesses do not divide; they let the ceremony proceed with dreadful calm, a rejected king keeping holy feast while plotting murder, and an empty chair beginning, without a word, to ask its question.
25:
The empty seat is noticed but not yet challenged, and the witnesses fasten on the seating of the table and on the difficult clause about Jonathan's rising. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the scene, Saul in his seat by the wall as at other times, Jonathan rising and Abner taking his place at Saul's side, and David's place standing empty; and they give the reason for Saul's silence, that he thought something has befallen him, he is not clean, surely he is not clean (compare Lev. 15:16; Deut. 23:11). Charles Ellicott works at the disputed phrase and Jonathan arose: rejecting the Septuagint's variant as senseless, he follows Abarbanel and Rashi, that Jonathan, having seated himself after Saul and seen David's place empty, arose and seated Abner at Saul's side in the vacant place, so that the seat next the king might not be conspicuously empty, himself taking the seat on Saul's other side. He reads the verb as causative, and quotes Kitto's shrewd explanation of why Saul expected David at all: the king supposed that after the ecstasy at Naioth David would think his feelings wholly changed and would return, friendly as of old.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pictures the small table, that Saul, Jonathan, David, and Abner sat together, Saul in the chief seat next the wall; only four sat there, so the absence of any one would soon be noticed.

The reformed John Gill notes simply that the king sat upon the seat he usually sat on. The witnesses differ only over the mechanics of who rose and who sat, not over the sense; and beneath the seating arrangements they mark the ominous quiet of the first day. Saul says nothing, charitably supposing a ceremonial defilement has kept David away, for he knew, as the puritan reading observes, what conscience David made of the law and that he would rather keep from the holy feast than come unclean. The reader, who knows the empty chair means fear of murder and not fear of pollution, watches the king's charitable silence with dread, aware that it will not last a second day.
26:
Saul's silence on the first day is charitably explained, and the two witnesses who speak read his supposition as the natural resort of a man who cannot yet imagine the true reason for David's absence. The reformed John Gill gives the plain fact, that Saul said nothing that day about David's absence, took no notice of it, and made no remark. The king's quiet is not yet suspicion; he lets the empty chair pass without comment.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley supplies the content of Saul's unspoken thought: something has befallen David, some accident that has rendered him unclean and so unfit to partake of the feast, which consisted in part of the remainders of the peace offerings, according to the law (compare Lev. 7:20). An unclean man was unfit not only to eat of the holy things but to come into any company, much more the king's, lest he pollute others. Saul therefore reasons, quite reasonably, that ceremonial defilement, easily incurred and cleared by evening, must be the cause.

Though only two voices comment, and briefly, the verse is heavy with dramatic irony, and the wider witness of the chapter deepens it. As the puritan reading observes, Saul knew what conscience David made of the law, and that he would rather keep away from the holy feast than come in his uncleanness; so the very integrity of David furnishes the king with an innocent explanation for an absence that in truth is flight from his sword. The reader sees what Saul does not: that the chair is empty not because David fears to defile the feast but because David fears to lose his life at it. There is a bitter irony here that the witnesses do not miss, that the king's charity supplies a cover for the very man he means to kill, and that David's scrupulous conscience about the law becomes, in Saul's mouth, the innocent excuse for an absence born of terror. The witnesses let the first day close in this false peace, the king supposing a ritual accident, the son knowing the deadly truth, and the empty place waiting silently for the second day, when Saul's patience will fail and the real question, so long deferred, will at last be asked aloud.
27:
On the second day the empty seat can no longer be ignored, and the witnesses attend to Saul's pointed question and the scorn folded into it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the demand, Wherefore cometh not the son of Jesse to meat, neither yesterday nor to-day?, and note the grammar, that the second is a nominative joined to the verb, so the sense is the morrow after the new moon, the second day. Jonathan answers as arranged with David, and Keil observes the family detail behind the excuse, that it was David's elder brother, then at the head of the household, who ordered the sacrificial meal. Charles Ellicott reads Saul's psychology: David's continued absence on the second day awoke the king's suspicion, for he was aware that no accident of ceremonial defilement would keep David away two days together.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the contemptuous title: Saul calls him the son of Jesse in scorn, to mark the meanness of his origin and as not deigning to call him by his proper name; and to day is pressed because the uncleanness that came by chance usually lasted but a single day, so a second day's absence demanded another explanation.

The reformed John Gill lingers over the calendar question, why a second day was kept at all: the Targum speaks of an intercalation, and Abarbanel is clear that two days were observed in this month, though Gill allows that, since the remainder of the peace offerings might be eaten the next day, Saul and his guests might simply have met again for that purpose. The witnesses do not divide; they mark together the turn from charity to suspicion. The king who yesterday supposed an innocent defilement today names David with a sneer and asks a question that is already an accusation, and the scornful son of Jesse, refusing the honoured name David had won, betrays the contempt and jealousy that a single empty chair has been enough to draw at last into the open. There is a lesson in the very form of the address, that a heart full of hatred will not so much as pronounce the name of the one it hates, but reaches for a title that diminishes him; and the witnesses observe that this refusal to name David marks the point at which Saul's suppressed malice begins to speak in its own voice, needing only the smallest provocation to pass from a muttered sneer to a hurled spear.
28:
Jonathan gives the agreed answer, and the witnesses read his reply as the faithful execution of the plan laid in the field. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, David earnestly asked leave of me to go to Bethlehem, and carry the excuse into the next verse, where Jonathan quotes David's own words about the family sacrifice and the elder brother's summons. They note that the answer corresponds exactly to what David had proposed earlier, so that Jonathan neither adds nor omits, but delivers the message as arranged, and they mark how Saul's kindled anger will follow immediately upon it.

The reformed John Gill states the plain office of the verse, that Jonathan answered Saul in reply to his question and in order to excuse David. The excuse is not invented on the spot but rehearsed beforehand, the very words agreed between the friends, so that Jonathan speaks as David's advocate at the dangerous table.

Only two witnesses comment directly, and there is no dispute; the verse is a hinge, the moment when the plan meets its test. Yet the wider reading of the chapter gives it weight. Jonathan, as the wesleyan witnesses observe elsewhere, represents David to the king as a man wanting in no instance of respect and duty to the government, one who did not go without leave humbly asked and obtained from his superior officer. He covers his friend with a plausible and, on the puritan reading, largely true account, that David was keeping the feast among his relations at Bethlehem, sent for by his elder brother. In answering, Jonathan knowingly exposes himself, for he must sense that a defence of David may draw the king's wrath upon his own head. The witnesses let the verse stand as the last quiet moment before the storm: the son, faithful to his covenant, speaks for the absent friend, and in the very act of speaking sets a match to his father's jealousy, which will blaze out in the verses that follow. It is worth marking, the witnesses suggest, how much love costs Jonathan even here. He might have kept silent and saved himself the danger of pleading a suspect cause; instead he takes David's excuse upon his own lips and stakes his standing with the king on it. The covenant sworn in the field is already being honoured at the table, for Jonathan does not merely wish David well from a safe distance but risks the royal displeasure by becoming his advocate to his face.
29:
Jonathan repeats David's excuse in David's own words, and the witnesses examine the family feast it describes and a small textual variant. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that Jonathan answers the king in the way agreed, quoting the plea verbatim, and records that the Septuagint reads my brothers for my brother, altering the original because it did not understand the singular, Jesse the father being still alive. Ellicott follows Dean Payne Smith's suggestion that, since the ceremony was not a private gathering but one shared by the district, the brother, probably the eldest, was the convener who summoned the absent member of the house of Jesse.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the nature of the feast: such family sacrifices were festal, the beasts first offered to God and their blood poured out before him, after which all who were bidden ate of the flesh; this was a family entertainment at whose beginning God was peculiarly honoured. The excuse thus rests on a real and pious custom, whatever its truth in David's case.

The reformed John Gill fills out the plea's plausibility: David's family had a sacrifice in the city of Bethlehem where they lived, a peace offering for the new moon and an anniversary thanksgiving for the mercies of the year; and since David, though absent from the court feast, was keeping the solemnity in another place, his absence was the more excusable, the rather that he kept it with his own family in his own city. The witnesses do not divide; they let Jonathan's careful, filial-sounding excuse hang in the air, a reasonable and religious plea that any indulgent father would have granted. Its very reasonableness sets up the shock of what follows, for a request so modest and so pious ought to have been met with a word of leave, and instead it will be met with a torrent of rage. The excuse is honourable; the reception will expose the murderous heart it was meant to test. That, after all, was the design: a plea that any reasonable father would grant becomes, when refused with fury, an infallible measure of the mind that refuses it. The more innocent the request, the more damning the wrath it provokes, so that Saul's coming outburst will not merely wound Jonathan but convict Saul, proving beyond doubt that no fault in David but only hatred in the king lies behind the empty chair.
30:
Saul's fury breaks out, and the witnesses divide over the exact insult in thou son of the perverse rebellious woman. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott proposes a better rendering than the traditional slander of Jonathan's mother: the word rendered perverse is probably not a feminine adjective but an abstract noun, so the phrase runs thou son of perversity of rebellion, a common Hebraism for a man of perverse and refractory nature, following Clericus, Lange, and Payne Smith. This, he urges, avoids the extreme improbability that Saul insulted his own wife, which contradicts the Hebrew family spirit; and the clause about thy mother's nakedness is no slur on her chastity but simply an Oriental way of saying she will feel ashamed at having brought such a son into the world. He reads Saul's outburst as the cunning of the partly insane, who saw through the specious excuse that David suspected his design and stayed away, and that his own son sympathised with the friend for whom he apologised.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke canvasses the versions, the Vulgate's son of the woman who of her own accord forces the man and the Septuagint's son of the damsels who came of their own accord, and observes that were such the meaning the reproach would fall harder on Saul than on his son; he prefers to take the Hebrew as son of an unjust rebellion, that is, thou art a rebel against thy own father, and adds the shrewd moral that abusive language always argues a mean, weak, and malevolent heart, most sarcasms bearing as hard on the speaker as on their object. John Wesley reads the taunt of confusion as Saul's charge that Jonathan has no royal blood in his veins, since he could so tamely give up his crown to so contemptible a person.

The reformed John Gill states the plain occasion, that Saul's anger was kindled against Jonathan for giving David leave and for excusing him. The witnesses divide only over the target of the insult, whether it strikes at the mother literally or is an abstract reproach hurled at Jonathan himself; on the substance they agree that here Saul's malice, so long masked, tears off its disguise. A pious excuse has provoked a foul curse, and the passion that flares over an empty chair confirms to the reader, before ever Jonathan learns it, that the king is wholly given over to the murder David feared.
31:
Saul lays bare the fear that drives him, and the witnesses read as long as the son of Jesse liveth thou shalt not be established as the naked confession of a jealous king and the proof that murder was premeditated. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the thought that ever tortured Saul's diseased brain, that David, his own kind physician, his faithful soldier, and his son's dearest friend, was plotting basely against him; and he hears the fell purpose betrayed in he shall surely die, while the command send and fetch him unto me tells us the murder had been pre-arranged to take place at the feast, those rough soldier chiefs about the table ready at any moment to execute their master's savage behest.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the reading forward into Jonathan's reply and its violent answer: when Jonathan asked Wherefore shall he die? what hath he done? Saul was so enraged that he hurled his javelin at his own son, as once at David (compare 1 Sam. 18:11); and by that act Jonathan saw that his father had firmly resolved to put David to death.

The reformed John Gill spells out the king's calculation: while the son of Jesse lived, Jonathan, though heir to the crown, would not be sure of it; it would be precarious, and he stood in danger of being deprived of it at his father's death; so it was, in Saul's twisted logic, Jonathan's highest wisdom to deliver David up to be slain, and his greatest folly to protect and provide for him. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the verse strips the king's soul bare. What David had staked his life to discover is now shouted across a banquet: Saul means to kill him, and would make his own son the instrument. As the puritan reading marks, it is probable Saul knew that David had been anointed by the same hand that anointed himself, so that in fighting David he was fighting the counsel of God, and the fool was not Jonathan, who secured an interest in the man Heaven had chosen, but Saul, who thought to defeat the purpose of the Almighty.
32:
Jonathan answers his father's rage with a question of conscience, and the witnesses honour the courage and restraint of his reply. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the moment closely: Jonathan, remembering the effect of his quiet, earnest remonstrance on a former occasion, again tried to deprecate his father's unreasoning, jealous anger, but this time to no purpose; a paroxysm of madness seized Saul, who grasped the long spear leaning at his side and, with hate and fury in his eye, raised the great war-weapon to strike down his own son.

The reformed John Gill marks what Jonathan did and did not say. He made no answer to the charges of perverseness, rebellion, and folly brought against himself, bearing them with patience; but he could not bear to hear his dear friend spoken against and doomed to death, and so replied only to that: Wherefore shall he be slain? what hath he done? The question is at once a defence of David and an appeal to justice, demanding the crime that could warrant the sentence, and implying, since none can be named, that the sentence is murder.

Only two witnesses comment directly, and they do not differ; together they show a son at the summit of a hard virtue. As the puritan reading of the chapter observes, generous spirits can far more easily bear to be abused themselves than to hear their friends abused; Jonathan swallows the insults to his own name and his mother's, judging it becoming in an inferior to bear with meekness and silence the contempts put upon him in wrath and passion, when thou art the anvil, lie thou still. But when the talk turns from his honour to David's life, he will not be silent, and his brave what hath he done? stands as the one voice of righteousness at that murderous table. The witnesses see in his self-command and his courage together a rare picture of love that endures its own wounds and yet will not endure injustice to a friend, and they let his unanswerable question hang in the air, exposing the guilt of a king who can charge David with no fault and yet resolves upon his death.
33:
Saul hurls his spear at his own son, and the witnesses read the act as the final, unanswerable proof of his murderous resolve. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it plainly: Saul cast a javelin at Jonathan to smite him, whereby Jonathan knew that it was determined of his father to slay David, and rose from the table in fierce anger, eating no meat that second day of the month, grieved for David because his father had done him shame. The word for the king's resolve, they note, is a substantive meaning an unalterable determination, the same root as in Jonathan's earlier warning; the spear-cast has turned a feared possibility into a proved fact.

The reformed John Gill pauses over the weapon: it seems Saul always had a javelin or spear in his hand, which the custom of those times explains, for in many countries kings carried spears instead of sceptres and used them as such. He cites the observation that in the days of Romulus kings bore spears as an ensign of royalty, which the Greeks called sceptres, so that the very emblem of Saul's kingship becomes, in his hand, the instrument of attempted murder.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley drives home the terrible irony: Saul had seemed in great care that Jonathan should be established in his kingdom, and now he himself aims at his life. What fools, what worse than savage beasts, does anger make! The witnesses do not divide; with one voice they mark the madness of unbridled passion. As the puritan reading warns, anger is a short madness, and he that hates his brother is a murderer; how necessary, then, to put a hook in its nose and a bridle in its jaws. The king who a moment before had urged Jonathan to secure his throne by David's death now tries to kill the very son whose crown he professed to guard, proving that rage, once let loose, will devour even what it means to protect. Jonathan needs no arrow now; the flying spear has told him all, and he leaves the table in grief, certain at last of the truth David had sworn.
34:
Jonathan leaves the table, and the witnesses weigh whether his rising was chiefly anger or grief, and what wound cut deepest. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses the softer reading: we should probably understand the words rather of Jonathan's grief than of his anger, the latter clause explaining the former, for he was grieved for David; he was grieved for his father, and grieved for his friend. On this view the fierce heat of the text is the heat of sorrow more than of rage.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott keeps both together and traces their sources. The record runs true, in fierce anger: the son of Saul left the presence and appeared no more at that fatal feast. The hot anger was stirred first by the terrible insult offered him, the prince and heir, before the assembled great ones of Israel, the uplifted spear following the bitter words in an act not soon to be forgotten by the spectators; and secondly by the determined, relentless enmity of Saul against David, of whose stainless integrity and perfect loyalty Jonathan was firmly convinced. Yet Ellicott judges that the bitter wrong done to David his friend affected Jonathan most of all.

The reformed John Gill reads the plain occasion, that Jonathan arose in fierce anger, resenting his father's attempt to smite him and his fixed resolution to slay David. The witnesses differ only in emphasis, whether anger or grief predominated, and all agree the two were mingled; and they note that Jonathan ate no meat that day, for they were not to eat of the holy things in mourning. So the feast that Saul kept in murderous hypocrisy ends with its noblest guest fasting in sorrow. The son who came to the table honoured leaves it insulted and endangered, and the friend who was meant to be murdered there is instead defended by the one man willing to risk a spear for him. The witnesses let Jonathan's empty stomach and heavy heart preach the ruin of Saul's house: a father who would kill both his son and his son's friend, and a son whose love for that friend has cost him his father's love and nearly his life.
35:
Morning comes and Jonathan keeps his word, and the witnesses read the appointed meeting as the faithful performance of a dangerous promise and the prelude to a sorrowful parting. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest account. Here is Jonathan's faithful performance of his promise to give David notice of the success of his perilous experiment: he went at the time and place appointed, within sight of David's hiding place, sent his footboy to fetch the arrows he shot at random, and gave the fatal signal by shooting an arrow beyond the lad, Is not the arrow beyond thee?, a word David understood better than the boy. Then, dismissing the lad and finding the coast clear, he stole one minute's conversation with David after bidding him flee. Henry lingers over the parting, the most sorrowful of two friends who, for aught that appears, met but once more (compare 1 Sam. 23:16): David fell on his face and bowed three times with the reverence of a servant, they wept on each other's neck till David exceeded, and they comforted themselves in the covenant of friendship they had sworn in the name of the Lord for themselves and their heirs.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the meeting is not described at length, for all was done as pre-arranged, and, alas, everything had come to pass as David in his sad foresight had expected. The reformed John Gill simply fixes the time, the next morning, the morning of the third day of the month, when the sign was to be given.

The witnesses do not divide; they dwell together on Jonathan's fidelity and the pathos of the separation. Henry draws the gospel comfort out of it, that while we are at home in the body and absent from the Lord this is our stay, that he has made with us an everlasting covenant; and that Christians need not sorrow as those without hope, for being one with Christ they are one with each other, and shall meet in his presence ere long to part no more. So the shot arrow that spells danger becomes, in the witnesses' hands, an emblem of every faithful warning given in love, and the tearful parting a figure of the hope that outlasts all partings.
36:
Jonathan shoots and sends the boy running, and the witnesses take up a small puzzle: only one arrow is mentioned, though three were agreed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the action, that as the lad ran Jonathan shot an arrow to go out beyond him, so that the arrow flew farther than the boy had run; and they resolve the difficulty by grammar, noting that the singular form for arrow is used here with indefinite generality, the historian not thinking it necessary, after what he had already written, to state expressly that Jonathan shot three arrows one after another. The rare form of the word, they add, occurs only with disjunctive accents, as again in 2 Kings 9:24.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the same line and rejects a tempting alternative. Expositors are in a little difficulty here, since only one arrow is named though three were to be shot; but we cannot imagine, as some suggest, that Jonathan shortened the affair and shot only once because there was danger in delay, for had there been such need of haste the parting scene would have been cut even shorter. It is better, with Keil, to take the singular as standing in an indefinite general way, the author not troubling to repeat that three arrows were shot in succession.

The reformed John Gill supplies the homely detail, that Jonathan doubtless told the lad the mark he should shoot at, the stone Ezel, and bade him look about there for the arrows. The witnesses do not divide; they agree, against the harmonizing shortcut, that the single arrow of the narrative stands for the three of the plan, a matter of the historian's economy, not a change in Jonathan's conduct. Beneath the small grammatical question they feel the gathering sorrow: the arrow flies beyond the boy, the danger-word is about to be spoken, and the running child, all unknowing, becomes the innocent messenger of a friendship's heartbreak, carrying an arrow while the two men behind him carry a grief he cannot see. The witnesses are content to leave the harmonizing shortcut aside, for it would purchase a superficial neatness at the cost of the plan's own logic; better to trust the historian, who having once described the arrangement of three arrows need not weary the reader by repeating it. What matters is not the arithmetic of the shafts but the errand they perform, to speak a sentence of exile in the only language a watched field would permit, the flight of an arrow read by a man who knew its meaning.
37:
Jonathan speaks the fatal word, and the witnesses hear in Is not the arrow beyond thee? the coded sentence of David's exile. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the scene: when the boy came to the place of the shot arrow, Jonathan called after him, See, the arrow is beyond thee, farther off, and again Quickly, haste, do not stand still, that the lad might not see David, who was somewhere near, and the boy picked up the arrow and came to his master. They note the textual point, that the original singular for arrow is to be understood as before, the plural being a later correction; and they mark that the writer here anticipates the remark of the next verse, that the boy knew nothing of what had been arranged between Jonathan and David.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a small precision, that Jonathan cried after the lad while he was still coming to, that is, near, the place of the arrow, for the following words show he had not yet reached it. The urgency is real: Jonathan hurries the boy so that no eye may fall on the hidden man.

The reformed John Gill fixes the spot, the mark Jonathan had told the lad to shoot toward, whereabouts he might expect to find the arrow. Only three witnesses comment, and they do not differ; together they let the double meaning of the shouted words do their quiet, terrible work. To the boy, the arrow is beyond thee is a careless remark about a wild shot; to David, crouched within earshot, it is the appointed sentence, flee for thy life. The witnesses feel the cruelty of a message that must be spoken aloud yet cannot be spoken plainly, so that David learns his doom not in an embrace but from a distance, over a child's head, in words framed to sound like nothing at all. The very haste Jonathan urges upon the lad, make speed, stay not, is love working under cover, hurrying the innocent witness off the field so that the friends may have one moment together before the parting the arrow has just declared.
38:
Jonathan hurries the lad away, and the witnesses read his urgency as love clearing the field for a last, stolen meeting. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, that Jonathan cried after the lad, Make speed, haste, stay not, and the boy gathered up the arrows and came to his master; then Jonathan gave the boy his weapons and sent him to the town, that he might converse with David for a few seconds after his departure and take leave of him unobserved.
