They fold in the narrator's note that the boy knew nothing, only Jonathan and David knew the matter, so that the whole exchange passed over the child's head.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke hears a double sense in the words. Though addressed to the lad, make speed, haste, stay not were spoken to David, indicating that his life was at stake and only a prompt flight could save him. On this reading Jonathan's shouted command to the servant is also a whispered warning to the friend, a single sentence carrying two messages, an errand for the boy and a farewell for David.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott keeps to the plain motive: though Jonathan trusted his young armour-bearer to a degree, he hurried the attendant away that he might not see David, who was close by in hiding and would presently appear; the next clause expressly tells us how the meeting was unknown and unwitnessed, the youth sent to the city that Jonathan might be alone once more with David. The reformed John Gill reads it the same way, that Jonathan, observing no one passing by, was eager to seize the chance for a few minutes' interview with David alone before he fled. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that everything now bends toward secrecy and haste, the innocent boy dispatched, the field cleared, the last precious moment secured. Whether Clarke's double meaning is pressed or not, all see in Jonathan's quick, insistent words the tenderness of a friend who will not let his companion vanish without one embrace, and who bends even the pretence of the archery game to buy a few seconds of farewell.
39:
The narrator pauses to say that the lad understood nothing, and the single witness who comments lets the verse's own emphasis stand. The reformed John Gill gives the plain sense, that the boy knew nothing of what was meant by the shooting of the arrows, by their being shot beyond where he stood, or by the urgent command to make haste and bring them; only Jonathan and David knew the matter. The whole elaborate device had been contrived precisely for this, that its meaning should be sealed from the very servant who carried it out.

Though only Gill speaks directly, the verse crowns the wisdom of the plan the wider company of witnesses has admired throughout. The grammatical-historical reading of the neighbouring verses has repeatedly stressed that the lad was sent and hurried away just so that he might not see David nor grasp the secret; and the point of the arrows, the shouted words about a careless shot, and the errand into the city was to pass a message of life and death over an uncomprehending head. Here the narrator states outright what the arrangement was designed to secure: an innocent instrument who could be trusted with an arrow because he could not read a sign.

The verse is brief, but it carries a quiet moral the witnesses feel keenly. The safety of David hung on secrecy, and secrecy was kept not by a confederate sworn to silence but by a child who simply did not understand. There is something both shrewd and tender in it: shrewd, that Jonathan entrusted the mechanics to one who could betray nothing because he knew nothing; tender, that two men bound in a covenant of love should have to conduct their farewell in a code, their deepest matter hidden even from the boy at their feet. The narrator's aside thus underlines both the peril and the pathos of the scene. The friends are alone with their secret; the world, represented by the little lad trudging back to the city with his master's bow, walks off unaware that it has just been the unwitting messenger of a kingdom's turning point.
40:
Jonathan sends his weapons back with the boy, and the witnesses fasten, almost with one voice, on the quaint word artillery and its true meaning. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes that this is, he believes, the only place in the language where the word artillery is not applied to cannon or ordnance; the original simply signifies instruments, and here means the bow, quiver, and arrows. The archaic English of the version, he notes, has fixed a word on the passage that a later age would render otherwise.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott says the same, that the word literally means his implements; the term artillery, expressive though it be, would scarcely now be used in this sense, and we should render the Hebrew by arms. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley glosses it briefly to the same effect, that the artillery was Jonathan's bow, arrows, and quiver.

The reformed John Gill renders it his vessels or instruments, his arms, as the Targum has it, the quiver, bow, and arrows. The witnesses do not divide at all; they agree entirely that the word carries no thought of gunnery but only of a bowman's gear, and their shared philological note, small as it is, serves the narrative's larger purpose. By handing his weapons to the lad and sending him back to the city, Jonathan strips himself of every encumbrance and every witness, so that he may be wholly alone and unarmed with his friend. The gesture is deliberate: the boy carries off the bow that shot the signal, and with it the last observer, leaving the two men free for the parting the arrows have announced. The witnesses let the homely detail of the returned weapons prepare the tender scene that follows, in which the instruments of the coded message are cleared away and only the friends remain, face to face, for their tears and their farewell. What was a device of concealment now yields to a moment of naked grief. There is a quiet fitness in it that the commentators feel: the bow and quiver that had just served to hide a message go back to the city on a boy's shoulder, and with them goes the last need for pretence. The elaborate machinery of the sign has done its work and is dismantled; nothing artificial stands any longer between the two men. Stripped of weapons and witnesses alike, Jonathan waits unarmed for the friend he may never see again, and the scene turns from cunning contrivance to the plain sorrow of parting.
41:
The boy gone, David comes out of hiding for the farewell, and the witnesses attend to his obeisance, the friends' weeping, and a knot in the text. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it, that when the boy had gone David rose from his hiding place on the south side, fell on his face to the ground and bowed three times before Jonathan; then they kissed each other and wept, till David wept strongly, to such a degree that he wept aloud. They defend the difficult phrase from the side of the south as the description of David's hiding place in relation to where Jonathan stood, a reading only Aquila and Jerome among the ancients rendered rightly, the other versions merely repeating the earlier verse because they could not see its force.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees the text here is very doubtful, but takes it, if correct, to mean that as soon as David saw Jonathan was alone he rose from the south side of the rock where he had lain concealed; the arrow-sign alone would have warned him, but seeing his friend waiting he came out. On the threefold obeisance he cites Josephus, that David did obeisance, and called him the saviour of his life; and on the strange closing words, until David exceeded, he offers that David either wept more bitterly than Jonathan or, in Payne Smith's phrase, broke down, completely mastered by his grief.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reflects on why David's distress was the greater: besides losing the friend he now thought to lose for ever, he lost his wife, relatives, country, and, most afflictive, the altars of his God and the ordinances of religion. Yet Clarke praises David's patience under providence, that though he could have raised a powerful party in Israel he left it to God to bring him to the throne in his own way and time, for he that believeth shall not make haste, and he who runs before he is sent runs without light and without divine strength. The reformed John Gill simply marks the moment, as soon as the lad was gone, which David could observe from his lurking place. The witnesses do not divide; over the obscure Hebrew they agree on the plain and moving sense, two friends prostrate and weeping in a lonely field, the outlaw's grief overflowing all bounds as he bows to the prince and turns to a homeless life.
42:
The friends part with a word and an oath, and the witnesses read Go in peace as the tender, broken close of one of Scripture's noblest friendships. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render Jonathan's parting: Go in peace. What we two have sworn in the name of the Lord, saying, The Lord be between me and thee, and between my seed and thy seed for ever, sc. let it stand, let us abide by it. The clause, they explain, is an aposiopesis, a sentence left unfinished from Jonathan's deep emotion, whose sense must be gathered from the context; and the words for ever, as a comparison with the earlier verse shows, belong to the substance of the oath. Then David set out on his journey and Jonathan returned to the town, a verse that, they add, should strictly close the chapter.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the same broken feeling: the abruptness of the closing words is most natural and accords with the deep emotion of the speaker; David's heart was too full to reply, and, blinded with tears, he seems to have hurried away speechless. Ellicott crowns the scene with Phillipson's tribute, that we may wonder at the delicacy and gentleness these two men entertained for one another in those old rough times, and that no ancient writer has set before us so noble an example of a heartfelt, unselfish, thoroughly human friendship, or described it with such profound knowledge of the human heart.

The reformed John Gill unfolds the blessing, that David was to go in peace of mind, committing himself, his family, and his affairs to the providence of God, who would keep him safe from Saul's designs, and to rest easy about the covenant between them, not doubting it would be faithfully observed on both sides. The witnesses do not divide; with one accord they let the unfinished sentence stand as the perfect close, for the oath that binds the two houses for ever needs no completing clause, only the silence of two hearts that understand each other. So the chapter that opened with a fugitive's protest of innocence ends with a covenant sealed in tears, the Lord set for ever between the friends and their seed, and David going out into exile carrying, in place of home and altar, the one possession that Saul could not take from him, a friendship sworn under the eye of God.
